---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: true
    pdf-engine: xelatex
incremental: false
header-includes: |
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
  \usepackage{tikz}
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric, fit, backgrounds, calc}
  \usepackage{booktabs}
  \usepackage{xcolor}
  \definecolor{mblue}{RGB}{35,55,100}
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="beamer"}
```{=latex}
\title{Clinical Research Methodology\\
\vspace{0.5em}
{\normalsize \textbf{Summary}}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}
\titlegraphic{
\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{../Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\hfill \includegraphics[scale=0.15]{../Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
}
\maketitle
```
:::

## 1) Tests and Psychometrics

For evidence-based psychological/neuropsychological practice, it is essential to have **measurements**.

**Tests** are tools for psychological measurement. Measurement and tests are objects of study of **psychometrics**.

When using tests in clinical practice, we face a particular problem: making inferences at the level of the **single case**, which requires special attention.

## Applied Psychometrics

The knowledge of **applied psychometrics** comes into play essentially in two moments:

- when we select the tests to use

- when we interpret the test results


## Psychometric Properties of Tests

Some important considerations are:

```{=latex}
\begin{itemize}
\item Consider \textbf{all the psychometric properties of a test}

\begin{itemize}
  \item validity
  \item reliability
  \item normative data
  \item change over time
  \item \ldots
\end{itemize}

\item in the transformation of scores to percentiles (and possible comparison with thresholds) remember that they respond to the question:

\begin{itemize}
\item How "strange" is it to observe this performance in my sample/normative population?
\end{itemize}

\end{itemize}

```

## Psychometrics and Scientific Evidence

![](../Figures/triangle){width="10%"}. In general remember that a **test** $\nRightarrow$ **construct**.

**It depends on the validity of the test!** (which could also change e.g. with cognitive damage)

## Scientific Studies

For evidence-based psychological/neuropsychological practice, it is essential to base yourself on the results of scientific studies.

The nature of the evidence can be **correlational** (all observational studies and some interventional ones) or **causal** (randomized clinical trials).

## Experimental Studies and Trials

**Clinical trials**, even though they are the principal method for assessing the efficacy of a treatment, *are not without limitations*.

In clinical psychology and clinical neuropsychology, this is mainly due to:

- bias in selection.
- difficulty in having an appropriate control group.
- difficulty in achieving a _double-blind_.


## Observational Studies

Observational studies provide important information, but there is always the risk lurking around the corner of making causal interpretations, _especially when these are plausible_.

However, this does not exclude the possibility of alternative explanations.

## Problems in Observational Studies

In observational studies there is often a tendency to think that the ideal thing is to "control" for covariates or insert variables in the model to isolate a predictor. However, this can even create errors if you do not consider certain effects (e.g., **colliders**)

## Evidence Synthesis

Also in evidence synthesis be careful about

"garbage in" $\to$ "garbage out"

## \small How Can Clinical Personnel Contribute to Research? (2/2)
\small
- Remembering that to contribute to research **it is not necessary to be a full-time researcher**: even those who work mainly in clinical settings can have a decisive role in improving the quality of available knowledge.

- Actively collaborating with researchers, not just as simple "data providers", but by **contributing to defining and refining the research question** based on real needs observed in clinical practice.

- **Helping to identify which problems are truly relevant for patients and for daily work**, avoiding studies that answer unhelpful questions or are far from clinical reality.

- **Participating in the collection of quality data**, accurate and standardized, a fundamental element for producing reliable results.

## \small How Can Clinical Personnel Contribute to Research? (2/2)
\small
- Contributing to the **interpretation of results**, because the clinical perspective is essential to understand whether a statistically significant result also has real practical value.

- Promoting collaboration between departments, centers and different professionals, so as to build larger and more representative datasets.

- **Favoring multicenter projects, large clinical databases** (possibly open and shareable) and well-designed trials, rather than small isolated studies that risk producing fragile or unhelpful results.

- **Promoting a culture of critical evaluation of evidence**, helping colleagues and patients to interpret research results with prudence and **awareness**.
